questions.question: Which organelle is known as the 'powerhouse of the cell' because it produces ATP through cellular respiration?
questions.explanation: The mitochondrion is called the powerhouse of the cell because it is the site of aerobic respiration, producing ATP (adenosine triphosphate) used for cellular energy.

questions.question: Which of the following structures is found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells?
questions.explanation: Plant cells have a rigid cell wall made of cellulose, which animal cells lack. Both cell types have mitochondria, cell membranes, and nuclei.

questions.question: The organelle responsible for protein synthesis in a cell is the:
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis (translation). They read mRNA and assemble amino acids into polypeptide chains.

questions.question: The fluid-filled sac that maintains turgor pressure in plant cells is the:
questions.explanation: The central vacuole in plant cells stores water and maintains turgor pressure, which keeps plant cells firm and supports the plant structure.

questions.question: Which organelle contains chlorophyll and is responsible for photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, the green pigment that captures light energy for photosynthesis. They are found only in plant cells and algae.

questions.question: The control centre of the cell that contains genetic information (DNA) is the:
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of eukaryotic cells, containing the cell's DNA organized into chromosomes. It controls cell activities and heredity.

questions.question: Prokaryotic cells differ from eukaryotic cells in that prokaryotic cells:
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells (bacteria and archaea) lack a membrane-bound nucleus. Their DNA is found in the cytoplasm as a circular chromosome.

questions.question: The Golgi apparatus is responsible for:
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus (Golgi body) receives proteins from the endoplasmic reticulum, modifies them, and packages them for secretion or intracellular use.

questions.question: Lysosomes are organelles that contain digestive enzymes used to:

options.C: break down waste materials and cellular debris

questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain hydrolytic enzymes that digest worn-out organelles, food particles, and foreign invaders through a process called autolysis.

questions.question: The semi-permeable membrane that controls what enters and leaves the cell is the:
questions.explanation: The cell (plasma) membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the movement of substances in and out of the cell. It is made of a phospholipid bilayer.

questions.question: The type of cell division that produces genetically identical daughter cells is called:
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid daughter cells. It is used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction.

questions.question: Meiosis results in the production of:
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces 4 haploid (n) daughter cells (gametes) with half the chromosome number of the parent cell. This maintains chromosome number across generations.

questions.question: The stage of mitosis during which chromosomes line up along the cell's equator (metaphase plate) is called:
questions.explanation: During metaphase, chromosomes align along the cell's equatorial plate (metaphase plate), attached to spindle fibres from both poles.

questions.question: During which phase of meiosis does crossing over (exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes) occur?
questions.explanation: Crossing over occurs during Prophase I of meiosis, when homologous chromosomes pair up (synapsis) and exchange segments at chiasmata, increasing genetic variation.

questions.question: A human body cell contains 46 chromosomes. After meiosis, each gamete would contain:
questions.explanation: Meiosis halves the chromosome number. Human body cells are diploid (2n=46), so gametes are haploid (n=23).

questions.question: Gregor Mendel's Law of Segregation states that:

options.A: genes for different traits assort independently
options.B: two alleles for each trait separate during gamete formation
options.C: dominant alleles always mask recessive ones

questions.explanation: The Law of Segregation states that the two alleles for a trait separate (segregate) during gamete formation, so each gamete carries only one allele for each gene.

questions.question: In a cross between two heterozygous tall plants (Tt × Tt), what is the expected ratio of tall to short offspring?
questions.explanation: Tt × Tt gives TT:Tt:tt = 1:2:1, so 3 tall (TT + 2Tt) : 1 short (tt). The phenotypic ratio is 3:1.

questions.question: An organism with two identical alleles for a trait (e.g., TT or tt) is said to be:
questions.explanation: A homozygous organism has two identical alleles for a gene (TT or tt). A heterozygous organism has two different alleles (Tt).

questions.question: The physical appearance of an organism resulting from its genetic makeup is called its:
questions.explanation: Phenotype is the observable physical characteristics of an organism (e.g., tall, blue eyes). Genotype is the genetic makeup (e.g., Tt, BB).

questions.question: Haemophilia is a sex-linked disorder. If a carrier female (X^H X^h) mates with a normal male (X^H Y), what percentage of their sons will have haemophilia?
questions.explanation: Sons get Y from father and either X^H or X^h from mother. 50% of sons get X^h Y (haemophiliac) and 50% get X^H Y (normal).

questions.question: The ABO blood group system in humans is an example of:
questions.explanation: ABO blood groups involve three alleles (I^A, I^B, i), with I^A and I^B being codominant (both expressed in AB blood type) and i being recessive.

questions.question: Which of the following blood groups is the universal donor?
questions.explanation: Blood group O is the universal donor because it has no A or B antigens on red blood cells, so it won't trigger an immune response in any recipient.

questions.question: Sickle cell anaemia is caused by a mutation in which gene?
questions.explanation: Sickle cell anaemia is caused by a point mutation in the haemoglobin gene, substituting valine for glutamic acid at position 6 of the beta-globin chain.

questions.question: Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment states that:

options.B: alleles for different genes assort independently during gamete formation

questions.explanation: The Law of Independent Assortment states that alleles for different genes segregate independently of one another during gamete formation (applies to genes on different chromosomes).

questions.question: A mutation is defined as:

options.A: the normal variation within a species
options.B: a permanent change in the DNA sequence of a gene or chromosome
options.D: the exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes

questions.explanation: A mutation is a heritable change in the nucleotide sequence of DNA. Mutations can be beneficial, harmful, or neutral.

questions.question: Darwin's theory of evolution is based on the concept of:
questions.explanation: Darwin proposed that evolution occurs through natural selection — organisms with traits better suited to their environment survive and reproduce more successfully.

questions.question: The concept that organisms that are better adapted to their environment survive and reproduce is called:
questions.explanation: Natural selection is the process by which individuals with favourable heritable traits have better reproductive success, leading to those traits becoming more common in populations.

questions.question: Evidence for evolution includes all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Fossil records, comparative anatomy (homologous structures), and molecular biology (DNA similarities) are direct evidence for evolution. Complexity alone is not evidence for evolution.

questions.question: Structures that have the same basic structural plan but are adapted for different functions are called:
questions.explanation: Homologous structures share a common ancestry and basic structural plan (e.g., human arm, whale flipper, bat wing) but may serve different functions.

questions.question: Vestigial organs are structures that:

options.A: are fully functional and important for survival
options.C: are reduced and functionless remnants of structures that were useful in ancestors

questions.explanation: Vestigial organs are reduced, functionless remnants of structures that served a purpose in ancestral organisms. Examples include the human appendix and tailbone (coccyx).

questions.question: The theory that proposed that acquired characteristics gained during an organism's lifetime could be inherited by offspring was proposed by:
questions.explanation: Lamarck proposed the inheritance of acquired characteristics — for example, that giraffes stretched their necks during their lifetime and passed longer necks to offspring. This theory was later disproved.

questions.question: The total number of organisms of the same species living in a given area at a given time is called a:
questions.explanation: A population is a group of individuals of the same species living in the same area at the same time and capable of interbreeding.

questions.question: All the populations of different species living and interacting in a given area constitute a:
questions.explanation: A community includes all the populations of different species (plants, animals, fungi, microorganisms) that live and interact in a given area.

questions.question: A food chain always begins with a:
questions.explanation: A food chain always starts with a producer (autotroph/plant) that fixes energy from the sun through photosynthesis.

questions.question: In a food chain: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle, the frog is a:
questions.explanation: The frog eats the grasshopper (primary consumer), making the frog a secondary consumer (second-level consumer).

questions.question: Organisms that break down dead organic matter into simpler inorganic substances are called:
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organisms and waste products, recycling nutrients back into the ecosystem.

questions.question: The relationship between a clownfish and a sea anemone, where the fish gets protection and the anemone gets cleaned, is an example of:
questions.explanation: Mutualism is a symbiotic relationship where both organisms benefit. The clownfish is protected by the anemone's tentacles and the clownfish cleans the anemone.

questions.question: Parasitism is a relationship in which:

options.B: one organism benefits while the other is harmed
options.C: one organism benefits while the other is unaffected

questions.explanation: In parasitism, the parasite benefits while the host is harmed. Examples include tapeworms in humans and mistletoe on trees.

questions.question: The nitrogen cycle involves the conversion of atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) into usable forms by:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria (e.g., Rhizobium in legume root nodules and Azotobacter in soil) convert atmospheric N₂ into ammonia (NH₃) that plants can use.

questions.question: The process by which energy flows through an ecosystem from producers to consumers shows that approximately what percentage of energy is transferred between each trophic level?
questions.explanation: The 10% rule states that approximately 10% of energy stored in one trophic level is transferred to the next. The rest is lost as heat through respiration.

questions.question: The role or function of an organism in its community, including its feeding habits and interactions with other organisms, is called its:
questions.explanation: An ecological niche is the role an organism plays in its ecosystem, including its position in the food web, habitat, and interactions with other species.

questions.question: Eutrophication of water bodies is caused by excess:

options.C: nutrients (nitrates and phosphates) from agricultural runoff

questions.explanation: Eutrophication occurs when excess nitrates and phosphates (from fertilizers) enter water bodies, causing algal blooms that deplete oxygen and kill aquatic life.

questions.question: Photosynthesis occurs in which part of the plant?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis primarily occurs in the leaves, specifically in the chloroplasts of mesophyll cells, where chlorophyll captures light energy.

questions.question: The overall equation for photosynthesis is:
questions.explanation: The equation for photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ (glucose) + 6O₂. Plants use CO₂, water, and light to produce glucose and oxygen.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a product of photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide is a reactant (raw material) in photosynthesis, not a product. Products include glucose and oxygen.

questions.question: The test for starch in a leaf involves using:
questions.explanation: Iodine solution turns blue-black in the presence of starch. This is used in the standard test for photosynthesis in leaves.

questions.question: Which nutrient is tested for using Benedict's reagent, which turns brick-red when positive?
questions.explanation: Benedict's reagent tests for reducing sugars (glucose, maltose, lactose). A positive result gives a brick-red precipitate after heating.

questions.question: The Biuret test is used to detect the presence of:
questions.explanation: The Biuret test detects proteins. A positive result gives a violet/purple colour when NaOH and dilute CuSO₄ are added to a protein solution.

questions.question: The emulsion test is used to detect the presence of:
questions.explanation: The emulsion test detects lipids. When ethanol is added to a substance containing fat and then water is added, a milky-white emulsion forms.

questions.question: Vitamins are classified as essential nutrients because the human body:

options.A: uses them as a major energy source
options.B: can manufacture them in large quantities
options.C: cannot synthesize sufficient amounts and must obtain them from diet
options.D: uses them to build cell membranes

questions.explanation: Vitamins are essential micronutrients that the body cannot produce in adequate amounts and must be obtained from food. They regulate metabolic processes.

questions.question: Deficiency of Vitamin C causes:
questions.explanation: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) deficiency causes scurvy, characterized by bleeding gums, slow wound healing, and skin problems.

questions.question: Deficiency of Vitamin D causes:
questions.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency impairs calcium absorption, causing rickets (soft, deformed bones) in children and osteomalacia (weak bones) in adults.

questions.question: Night blindness is caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Vitamin A (retinol) is needed to produce rhodopsin (visual purple) in rod cells. Deficiency leads to night blindness and eventually total blindness (xerophthalmia).

questions.question: The main function of red blood cells (erythrocytes) is to:
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds oxygen in the lungs and releases it to body tissues. They lack a nucleus to maximize space for haemoglobin.

questions.question: The liquid component of blood in which blood cells are suspended is called:
questions.explanation: Blood plasma is the straw-coloured liquid (about 55% of blood volume) that carries blood cells, proteins, nutrients, hormones, and waste products.

questions.question: The blood vessels that carry oxygenated blood away from the heart are called:
questions.explanation: Arteries carry blood away from the heart. Most arteries carry oxygenated blood (except the pulmonary artery, which carries deoxygenated blood to the lungs).

questions.question: In plants, water and mineral salts are transported from the roots to the leaves through:
questions.explanation: Xylem vessels transport water and dissolved mineral salts upward from roots to leaves through a process driven by transpiration pull.

questions.question: In plants, manufactured food (sucrose and amino acids) is transported from leaves to other parts through:
questions.explanation: Phloem sieve tubes transport organic solutes (mainly sucrose) from leaves (source) to other parts of the plant (sink) through translocation.

questions.question: The process by which water is lost from plant leaves through stomata is called:
questions.explanation: Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from plant surfaces (mainly through leaf stomata). It creates the 'transpiration pull' that draws water up the xylem.

questions.question: Osmosis is defined as the movement of water molecules from a region of:

options.A: low water concentration to high water concentration through a selectively permeable membrane
options.B: high water concentration to low water concentration through a selectively permeable membrane
options.C: high solute concentration to low solute concentration

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from a region of high water concentration (dilute solution) to low water concentration (concentrated solution) through a selectively permeable membrane.

questions.question: A plant cell placed in a hypertonic solution will:
questions.explanation: In a hypertonic solution (more concentrated than cell sap), water leaves the cell by osmosis, causing the cell to shrink and the membrane to pull away from the wall (plasmolysis).

questions.question: The overall equation for aerobic respiration is:
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP (energy). Glucose is completely oxidized using oxygen to produce CO₂, water, and energy.

questions.question: Anaerobic respiration in yeast produces:
questions.explanation: Anaerobic respiration in yeast (fermentation): C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂. Ethanol and carbon dioxide are produced without using oxygen.

questions.question: Anaerobic respiration in mammalian muscle cells produces:
questions.explanation: During intense exercise when oxygen is insufficient, mammalian muscle cells produce lactic acid through anaerobic respiration, causing muscle fatigue.

questions.question: The first stage of cellular respiration that takes place in the cytoplasm is called:
questions.explanation: Glycolysis is the first stage of cellular respiration, occurring in the cytoplasm. Glucose (6C) is broken down into two pyruvate molecules (3C), producing small amounts of ATP.

questions.question: The main nitrogenous waste product excreted by humans is:
questions.explanation: Humans excrete urea as the main nitrogenous waste. Urea is produced in the liver from the breakdown of excess amino acids (deamination) and excreted by the kidneys.

questions.question: The functional unit of the kidney is called the:
questions.explanation: The nephron is the structural and functional unit of the kidney. Each kidney contains about 1 million nephrons that filter blood and form urine.

questions.question: The process by which small molecules are forced from the blood into the Bowman's capsule of the kidney is called:
questions.explanation: Ultrafiltration occurs at the glomerulus (Bowman's capsule). Blood pressure forces small molecules (water, glucose, urea, salts) out of the blood into the capsule.

questions.question: In the kidney, glucose is normally reabsorbed in the:
questions.explanation: All glucose filtered by the glomerulus is normally reabsorbed in the proximal convoluted tubule through active transport. Glucose in urine (glycosuria) indicates diabetes mellitus.

questions.question: The organ that performs excretion of urea in humans is the:
questions.explanation: The kidney excretes urea (produced in the liver), excess water, and salts as urine. The liver produces urea but the kidney excretes it.

questions.question: Carbon dioxide is mainly excreted from the human body through the:
questions.explanation: CO₂, a waste product of cellular respiration, is transported in blood to the lungs and exhaled. The lungs are the main organs of CO₂ excretion.

questions.question: In flowering plants, pollination is the transfer of pollen from the:
questions.explanation: Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther (male part) to the stigma (female part) of a flower, either of the same or different flower.

questions.question: In humans, fertilization normally takes place in the:
questions.explanation: Fertilization (fusion of sperm and egg) normally occurs in the Fallopian tube (oviduct). The fertilized egg (zygote) then travels to the uterus for implantation.

questions.question: The hormone responsible for stimulating egg release (ovulation) in females is:
questions.explanation: A surge in LH (Luteinizing Hormone) triggers ovulation — the release of a mature egg from the Graafian follicle around day 14 of the menstrual cycle.

questions.question: The function of the placenta in mammals is to:

options.B: allow exchange of materials between mother and foetus

questions.explanation: The placenta allows oxygen, nutrients, and antibodies to pass from mother to foetus, while CO₂ and metabolic wastes pass from foetus to mother.

questions.question: Asexual reproduction in Amoeba occurs by:
questions.explanation: Amoeba reproduces asexually by binary fission — the cell divides into two equal daughter cells, each genetically identical to the parent.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of vegetative reproduction in plants?
questions.explanation: Vegetative reproduction is asexual reproduction in plants. Bulbs (onion), tubers (potato), runners (strawberry), and rhizomes (ginger) are all examples.

questions.question: The male sex hormone produced by the testes is:
questions.explanation: Testosterone is the primary male sex hormone produced by the Leydig cells of the testes. It controls development of male secondary sexual characteristics.

questions.question: In flowering plants, the part that develops into the seed after fertilization is the:
questions.explanation: After fertilization, the ovule develops into the seed while the ovary wall develops into the fruit. The seed contains the embryo.

questions.question: Double fertilization in flowering plants involves the fusion of:

options.A: two sperm cells with two eggs
options.B: one sperm with the egg to form a zygote, and another sperm with the polar nuclei to form the endosperm
options.D: two polar nuclei with the ovule

questions.explanation: In double fertilization (unique to angiosperms), one sperm fertilizes the egg (forming the diploid zygote) and the other fuses with the two polar nuclei (forming the triploid endosperm).

questions.question: The basic structural and functional unit of the nervous system is the:
questions.explanation: The neuron (nerve cell) is the basic unit of the nervous system. It consists of a cell body, dendrites (receiving signals), and an axon (transmitting signals).

questions.question: A reflex action is best described as a:

options.A: voluntary, conscious response to a stimulus
options.B: rapid, automatic, involuntary response to a stimulus

questions.explanation: A reflex action is a rapid, automatic, involuntary response to a stimulus that does not involve conscious thought (e.g., knee-jerk reflex, withdrawal from pain).

questions.question: The junction between two neurons where nerve impulses are transmitted is called a:
questions.explanation: A synapse is the junction between two neurons. Nerve impulses are transmitted across the synapse by chemical messengers called neurotransmitters (e.g., acetylcholine).

questions.question: The part of the brain responsible for coordination of movement and balance is the:
questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates voluntary movements, balance, and posture. Damage to the cerebellum causes ataxia (loss of coordination).

questions.question: The part of the nervous system that controls involuntary functions such as heartbeat, breathing, and digestion is the:
questions.explanation: The autonomic nervous system controls involuntary functions (heartbeat, breathing, digestion, glandular secretion). It has two divisions: sympathetic and parasympathetic.

questions.question: The hormone that regulates blood glucose levels by promoting glucose uptake into cells is:
questions.explanation: Insulin (produced by beta cells of the pancreatic islets) lowers blood glucose by promoting glucose uptake by cells and converting glucose to glycogen (glycogenesis).

questions.question: Diabetes mellitus is caused by:
questions.explanation: Diabetes mellitus results from insufficient insulin production (Type 1) or insulin resistance (Type 2), leading to persistently high blood glucose levels (hyperglycaemia).

questions.question: The 'fight or flight' hormone produced by the adrenal medulla is:
questions.explanation: Adrenaline (epinephrine) is released by the adrenal medulla in response to stress or fear, preparing the body for 'fight or flight' by increasing heart rate, blood sugar, and alertness.

questions.question: Thyroxine, produced by the thyroid gland, primarily controls:
questions.explanation: Thyroxine regulates the metabolic rate — the rate at which the body uses energy. Too little causes hypothyroidism (goitre); too much causes hyperthyroidism.

questions.question: Auxins are plant hormones that primarily promote:
questions.explanation: Auxins (e.g., indoleacetic acid, IAA) promote cell elongation, particularly in shoots. They cause phototropism (bending toward light) and gravitropism.

questions.question: The plant hormone that promotes ripening of fruits is:
questions.explanation: Ethylene (ethene) is a gaseous plant hormone that promotes fruit ripening. This is why placing ripe and unripe fruits together speeds up ripening.

questions.question: The type of joint that allows movement in all directions (e.g., shoulder joint) is called a:
questions.explanation: A ball and socket joint (e.g., shoulder, hip) allows movement in all planes and directions, giving the greatest range of motion.

questions.question: Muscles are connected to bones by:
questions.explanation: Tendons are tough bands of fibrous connective tissue that connect muscles to bones. Ligaments connect bone to bone.

questions.question: Bones are connected to other bones by:
questions.explanation: Ligaments are fibrous connective tissues that connect bones to other bones at joints, providing stability while allowing movement.

questions.question: The protein found in muscles that is responsible for contraction is:
questions.explanation: Muscle contraction occurs through the sliding filament mechanism involving actin (thin filaments) and myosin (thick filaments) proteins.

questions.question: The hierarchy of biological classification from largest to smallest grouping is:

options.A: Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species
options.B: Species, Genus, Family, Order, Class, Phylum, Kingdom
options.C: Kingdom, Class, Phylum, Order, Family, Genus, Species
options.D: Domain, Species, Genus, Family, Order, Class, Phylum

questions.explanation: Biological classification hierarchy (largest to smallest): Kingdom → Phylum → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species. Mnemonic: 'King Philip Came Over For Good Spaghetti.'

questions.question: The scientific name of an organism consists of its:
questions.explanation: Binomial nomenclature (Linnaeus) gives each organism a two-part scientific name: Genus (capitalized) + species (lowercase), e.g., Homo sapiens.

questions.question: Fungi differ from plants in that fungi:
questions.explanation: Fungi lack chlorophyll and cannot photosynthesize. They are heterotrophs that obtain nutrients by decomposing organic matter (saprophytes) or living on hosts (parasites).

questions.question: Viruses are not considered living organisms because they:

options.B: cannot reproduce outside a host cell and lack cellular organization
options.D: are too small to be seen

questions.explanation: Viruses are acellular (have no cell structure) and can only replicate inside living host cells. They lack organelles, metabolism, and independent reproduction.

questions.question: The kingdom that includes organisms such as Amoeba, Paramecium, and Euglena is:
questions.explanation: Kingdom Protista (Protoctista) includes unicellular eukaryotes such as Amoeba (Rhizopoda), Paramecium (Ciliophora), and Euglena (Euglenophyta).

questions.question: Malaria in humans is caused by a protozoan called:
questions.explanation: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium species (P. falciparum, P. vivax, etc.) transmitted by the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.

questions.question: The vector (carrier) of malaria is the:
questions.explanation: The female Anopheles mosquito is the vector of malaria. It transmits Plasmodium parasites when it bites a human to feed on blood.

questions.question: Tuberculosis (TB) is caused by which bacterium?
questions.explanation: Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, a bacterium that primarily affects the lungs. It is spread by airborne droplets from infected individuals.

questions.question: Which disease is caused by the Plasmodium falciparum and is particularly deadly?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum causes the most severe and often fatal form of malaria (cerebral malaria), responsible for most malaria deaths worldwide.

questions.question: Cholera is caused by which bacterium?
questions.explanation: Cholera is caused by Vibrio cholerae, transmitted through contaminated water and food. It causes severe watery diarrhoea and can lead to fatal dehydration.

questions.question: The HIV virus attacks which specific type of immune cell?
questions.explanation: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) specifically targets and destroys CD4+ T helper cells, progressively weakening the immune system and leading to AIDS.

questions.question: Antibiotics are effective against:
questions.explanation: Antibiotics kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria. They are NOT effective against viral infections. Misuse of antibiotics leads to antibiotic resistance.

questions.question: Vaccination provides immunity by:

options.A: introducing fully active pathogens into the body
options.B: stimulating the immune system to produce antibodies without causing disease
options.C: directly killing pathogens in the body

questions.explanation: Vaccines contain weakened, killed, or parts of pathogens that stimulate the immune system to produce memory cells and antibodies without causing the disease.

questions.question: Antibodies are produced by which type of white blood cell?
questions.explanation: B lymphocytes (B cells) differentiate into plasma cells that produce specific antibodies against antigens. T lymphocytes coordinate and carry out cell-mediated immunity.

questions.question: Passive immunity involves:

options.A: the body producing its own antibodies after vaccination
options.B: receiving ready-made antibodies from another source

questions.explanation: Passive immunity involves receiving ready-made antibodies (e.g., maternal antibodies through breast milk, or antitoxin injections). It gives immediate but short-lived protection.

questions.question: The place where an organism lives is called its:
questions.explanation: A habitat is the natural environment where an organism lives, including all physical and biological conditions. The niche is the role the organism plays.

questions.question: The water cycle involves which of the following processes?
questions.explanation: The water cycle involves evaporation (water → vapour), condensation (vapour → clouds), precipitation (rain/snow), and runoff/infiltration back into water bodies.

questions.question: An ecological pyramid of numbers shows:

options.A: the total biomass at each trophic level
options.B: the number of organisms at each trophic level
options.C: the amount of energy at each trophic level
options.D: the types of organisms in an ecosystem

questions.explanation: A pyramid of numbers shows the number of organisms at each trophic level. It can be inverted (e.g., one tree supporting many insects).

questions.question: Competition between organisms of the same species for resources is called:
questions.explanation: Intraspecific competition is competition between individuals of the same species. Interspecific competition occurs between different species.

questions.question: The gradual change in the composition of species in an ecosystem over time is called:
questions.explanation: Ecological succession is the process of gradual change in the composition of species in a community over time, from pioneer species to a stable climax community.

questions.question: In genetics, if a man with blood group AB marries a woman with blood group O, what blood groups are possible in their children?
questions.explanation: AB (I^A I^B) × OO (ii): possible offspring genotypes are I^A i (blood group A) and I^B i (blood group B). Only A and B are possible.

questions.question: Down syndrome is caused by the presence of an extra chromosome 21, a condition called:
questions.explanation: Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) results from the presence of three copies of chromosome 21 instead of two, usually due to non-disjunction during meiosis.

questions.question: The region of a chromosome where crossing over occurs and chromosomes exchange segments during meiosis I is called a:
questions.explanation: A chiasma (plural: chiasmata) is the point of exchange between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes during crossing over in Prophase I of meiosis.

questions.question: Incomplete dominance produces a blending of traits. A cross between a red flower (RR) and white flower (rr) will produce offspring that are:
questions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygous offspring (Rr) show an intermediate phenotype (pink) — a blend of both parent phenotypes, rather than one masking the other.

questions.question: The substance that makes up chromosomes and stores genetic information is:
questions.explanation: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is the hereditary material that makes up chromosomes and stores genetic information as a sequence of nucleotide bases.

questions.question: DNA replication is described as 'semi-conservative' because:

options.A: only half the DNA is copied each time
options.B: each new DNA molecule consists of one original and one new strand
options.C: DNA replication is only half completed before cell division
options.D: half of the bases are conserved

questions.explanation: Semi-conservative replication means each daughter DNA molecule retains one original (parental) strand and has one newly synthesized strand.

questions.question: The process by which information in DNA is used to make mRNA is called:
questions.explanation: Transcription is the process of copying the DNA code into mRNA (messenger RNA) in the nucleus, carried out by RNA polymerase.

questions.question: The process by which mRNA is used to synthesize a protein at the ribosome is called:
questions.explanation: Translation is the process of decoding mRNA to build a polypeptide (protein) at the ribosome, with tRNA bringing amino acids in the correct sequence.

questions.question: In DNA, adenine (A) always pairs with:
questions.explanation: Chargaff's rules: in DNA, A pairs with T (adenine-thymine) and G pairs with C (guanine-cytosine). In RNA, U (uracil) replaces T.

questions.question: A codon is a sequence of how many nucleotide bases in mRNA that codes for one amino acid?
questions.explanation: A codon is a triplet of three mRNA nucleotide bases (e.g., AUG, UUU) that codes for a specific amino acid. There are 64 possible codons for 20 amino acids.

questions.question: Homeostasis refers to the ability of an organism to:

options.B: maintain a stable internal environment despite external changes

questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the maintenance of a relatively stable internal environment (temperature, pH, blood glucose, water balance) despite changes in the external environment.

questions.question: The maintenance of constant body temperature in mammals is called:
questions.explanation: Thermoregulation is the process by which warm-blooded (endothermic) animals maintain a constant body temperature through mechanisms like sweating, shivering, and vasodilation/vasoconstriction.

questions.question: When body temperature rises above normal, which mechanism helps to cool the body?
questions.explanation: When body temperature rises, sweat glands produce sweat (evaporation cools skin) and blood vessels near the skin dilate (vasodilation) to release more heat.

questions.question: Digestion of starch begins in the:
questions.explanation: Starch digestion begins in the mouth where salivary amylase (ptyalin) breaks starch into maltose. It continues in the small intestine with pancreatic amylase.

questions.question: Proteins are digested mainly in the:
questions.explanation: Protein digestion begins in the stomach (pepsin in acidic conditions) and continues in the small intestine (trypsin, chymotrypsin from pancreas). End products are amino acids.

questions.question: The enzyme that breaks down fats (lipids) into fatty acids and glycerol is called:
questions.explanation: Lipase enzymes (e.g., pancreatic lipase) catalyze the hydrolysis of fats (triglycerides) into fatty acids and glycerol.

questions.question: Absorption of digested food mainly occurs in the:
questions.explanation: The small intestine is the main site of absorption of digested nutrients (amino acids, glucose, fatty acids) into the bloodstream and lymphatic system through villi.

questions.question: The finger-like projections in the small intestine that increase the surface area for absorption are called:
questions.explanation: Villi (singular: villus) are finger-like projections of the small intestinal wall that greatly increase the surface area for nutrient absorption. Microvilli are smaller projections on villi cells.

questions.question: Bile is produced by the liver and is important in digestion because it:

options.B: emulsifies fats to increase surface area for lipase action

questions.explanation: Bile (produced by the liver, stored in the gallbladder) emulsifies large fat droplets into smaller ones, increasing the surface area for lipase enzymes to act on.

questions.question: The carbon cycle involves which of the following processes?

options.B: Photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, combustion, and fossilization

questions.explanation: The carbon cycle involves photosynthesis (fixing CO₂), respiration (releasing CO₂), decomposition, combustion of fossil fuels, and fossilization of organic matter.

questions.question: Industrial melanism, observed in the peppered moth (Biston betularia) in England, is an example of:

options.B: Natural selection leading to a change in allele frequency

questions.explanation: Industrial melanism shows natural selection in action: dark moths became more common on soot-darkened trees because pale moths were more easily seen and eaten by predators.

questions.question: Analogous structures (e.g., wings of birds and insects) are evidence for:
questions.explanation: Analogous structures perform similar functions but have different evolutionary origins, demonstrating convergent evolution where different lineages independently evolve similar solutions.

questions.question: The corpus luteum forms from the Graafian follicle after ovulation and secretes:
questions.explanation: After ovulation, the empty Graafian follicle develops into the corpus luteum (yellow body), which secretes progesterone (and some oestrogen) to maintain the uterine lining for possible implantation.

questions.question: The fluid-filled sac that surrounds and protects the foetus in the uterus is the:
questions.explanation: The amnion is a membrane that forms the amniotic sac, filled with amniotic fluid that cushions and protects the developing embryo/foetus.

questions.question: The growth of a plant shoot toward light is called:
questions.explanation: Phototropism is the growth response of a plant to light. Shoots show positive phototropism (grow toward light), while roots show negative phototropism.

questions.question: The growth of roots downward in response to gravity is called:
questions.explanation: Roots show positive geotropism (gravitropism) — they grow toward the pull of gravity. Shoots show negative geotropism — they grow away from gravity.

questions.question: The small pores on the underside of leaves through which gaseous exchange and water vapour loss occur are called:
questions.explanation: Stomata (singular: stoma) are pores on leaves (mainly the lower surface) surrounded by guard cells that regulate their opening and closing for gaseous exchange and transpiration.

questions.question: The opening and closing of stomata is controlled by:
questions.explanation: Guard cells surround each stoma and control its opening (during the day when photosynthesis occurs) and closing (at night or during drought) through changes in turgor pressure.

questions.question: Germination of a seed requires all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Seeds need water, oxygen (for aerobic respiration), and suitable temperature for germination. Light is NOT required for germination (though it may be needed later for growth).

questions.question: Which of the following plant hormones inhibits growth and promotes dormancy and leaf fall?
questions.explanation: Abscisic acid (ABA) is a growth inhibitor that promotes seed and bud dormancy, stomatal closure during drought, and leaf abscission (leaf fall).

questions.question: In humans, gaseous exchange occurs in the:
questions.explanation: Gaseous exchange (O₂ in, CO₂ out) occurs in the alveoli — tiny air sacs in the lungs with thin walls, large surface area, and rich blood supply.

questions.question: In fish, gaseous exchange occurs through:
questions.explanation: Fish use gills for gaseous exchange. Water passes over the gill lamellae where oxygen is absorbed and CO₂ is released through diffusion.

questions.question: Insects breathe through a system of tubes called:
questions.explanation: Insects have a tracheal system — a network of air tubes (tracheae) that open to the outside through spiracles and carry oxygen directly to body tissues.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of all mammals?

options.C: They are warm-blooded and have mammary glands

questions.explanation: All mammals are endothermic (warm-blooded), have mammary glands for feeding young with milk, and have hair/fur. Not all have four limbs (e.g., snakes, whales).

questions.question: Amphibians are characterized by the ability to live:

options.C: both on land and in water, with aquatic larvae and terrestrial adults

questions.explanation: Amphibians (frogs, toads, salamanders) have aquatic larvae (tadpoles) and terrestrial adults. They need moisture because they breathe partly through their skin.

questions.question: Deforestation leads to all of the following EXCEPT:

options.D: increased carbon dioxide in the atmosphere

questions.explanation: Deforestation leads to reduced rainfall (less transpiration), soil erosion, biodiversity loss, and increased CO₂ as trees that absorb CO₂ are removed.

questions.question: Biodiversity refers to:

options.A: the number of individuals in a population
options.B: the variety of living organisms, genes, and ecosystems in an area
options.C: the total biomass in an ecosystem
options.D: the rate of energy flow in a food web

questions.explanation: Biodiversity encompasses the variety of life at all levels: genetic diversity within species, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity.

questions.question: If a woman who is a carrier of colour blindness (X^N X^n) marries a colour-blind man (X^n Y), what is the probability of their daughters being colour-blind?
questions.explanation: Daughters receive X from father (X^n) and X from mother (X^N or X^n with 50% probability each). So 50% of daughters will be X^n X^n (colour-blind).

questions.question: The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) exists in two forms. The rough ER is covered with:
questions.explanation: The rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) is studded with ribosomes that synthesize proteins destined for secretion or for use in membranes. The smooth ER lacks ribosomes.

questions.question: The microorganism responsible for causing typhoid fever is:
questions.explanation: Typhoid fever is caused by the bacterium Salmonella typhi, transmitted through contaminated food and water. Symptoms include high fever, abdominal pain, and rash.

questions.question: Ringworm is caused by:
questions.explanation: Despite its name, ringworm is not caused by a worm but by a group of fungi (dermatophytes) such as Trichophyton that infect skin, hair, and nails.

questions.question: The oxygen cycle is closely linked to which two biological processes?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis produces O₂ while respiration consumes O₂ and produces CO₂. These two processes maintain the balance of oxygen in the atmosphere.

questions.question: Kwashiorkor is a nutritional disorder caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Kwashiorkor is severe protein deficiency, common in children after weaning. It causes oedema (fluid swelling), wasted muscles, and an enlarged abdomen.

questions.question: Marasmus is a condition caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Marasmus is caused by severe deficiency of total calories (energy and protein), resulting in extreme wasting of muscles and fat. The child appears very thin and weak.

questions.question: In a dihybrid cross, two genes that are on different chromosomes assort independently. A cross AaBb × AaBb will give a phenotypic ratio of:
questions.explanation: A dihybrid cross AaBb × AaBb produces a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio (9 A_B_ : 3 A_bb : 3 aaB_ : 1 aabb) when the genes assort independently.

questions.question: Speciation is defined as the process by which:

options.A: individual organisms adapt to their environment
options.B: a new species arises from an existing species, usually through reproductive isolation

questions.explanation: Speciation is the evolutionary process by which populations diverge and develop reproductive isolation from each other, eventually forming distinct species.

questions.question: The cardiac cycle refers to the sequence of events in one complete heartbeat. The contraction phase of the heart is called:
questions.explanation: Systole is the contraction phase of the heartbeat when blood is pumped out of the heart. Diastole is the relaxation phase when the heart fills with blood.

questions.question: Diffusion is the movement of molecules from a region of:
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of molecules from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration (down a concentration gradient), requiring no energy.

questions.question: Active transport differs from diffusion in that active transport:

options.A: moves molecules from high to low concentration
options.C: moves molecules against the concentration gradient and requires energy (ATP)

questions.explanation: Active transport moves molecules against the concentration gradient (from low to high concentration) using energy from ATP. It requires carrier proteins.

questions.question: The muscle that plays the major role in breathing (ventilation) is the:
questions.explanation: The diaphragm is the primary muscle of respiration. When it contracts and flattens, it increases thoracic volume and air is drawn into the lungs (inhalation).

questions.question: The scientific name of humans is:
questions.explanation: The scientific name of modern humans is Homo sapiens (Latin: 'wise man'). We belong to the genus Homo and the species sapiens.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes a dicotyledonous plant?

options.A: Has one seed leaf (cotyledon) and parallel leaf veins
options.B: Has two seed leaves (cotyledons), net-veined leaves, and flower parts in fours or fives
options.C: Has fibrous roots and scattered vascular bundles

questions.explanation: Dicotyledons (dicots) have two cotyledons, net-veined leaves, flower parts in multiples of 4 or 5, and a ring of vascular bundles. Examples: beans, sunflower, mango.

questions.question: A test cross is performed to determine whether an organism showing a dominant phenotype is:
questions.explanation: A test cross crosses an organism with the dominant phenotype with a homozygous recessive individual. If any offspring show the recessive trait, the dominant-phenotype parent is heterozygous.

questions.question: Gibberellins promote which of the following in plants?

options.B: Stem elongation, seed germination, and flowering

questions.explanation: Gibberellins are plant hormones that promote stem elongation (internode elongation), seed germination, fruit development, and in some plants, flowering.

questions.question: The role of the large intestine in digestion is mainly to:
questions.explanation: The large intestine (colon) primarily absorbs water, mineral salts, and vitamins from undigested material, forming solid faeces for excretion.

questions.question: The organism that causes sleeping sickness (African trypanosomiasis) is transmitted by the:
questions.explanation: African sleeping sickness is caused by Trypanosoma brucei and transmitted by the tsetse fly (Glossina species). It affects the central nervous system.

questions.question: Penicillin was discovered by:
questions.explanation: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 when he observed that the mould Penicillium notatum inhibited bacterial growth on his culture plates.

questions.question: Which of the following is a biotic factor in an ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Biotic factors are living components of an ecosystem (organisms and their interactions): predation, competition, disease, parasitism. Abiotic factors are non-living (temperature, pH, light, humidity).

questions.question: The permanent removal of forests to convert land for other uses is called:
questions.explanation: Deforestation is the large-scale removal of forests. It leads to soil erosion, loss of biodiversity, disruption of water cycles, and increased CO₂ levels.

questions.question: The genotype for sickle cell trait (carrier) in humans is:
questions.explanation: HbA HbS is the sickle cell trait. These individuals are carriers — they have one normal (HbA) and one sickle cell (HbS) allele, and are largely symptom-free.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the root in a plant?

options.B: Anchorage of the plant in the soil
options.D: Storage of food in some plants

questions.explanation: Roots do not carry out photosynthesis because they lack chlorophyll and are usually underground (away from light). Roots absorb water/minerals, anchor the plant, and some store food.

questions.question: Self-pollination occurs when pollen is transferred to the stigma of:

options.B: the same flower or another flower on the same plant
options.C: a flower on a different plant of the same species

questions.explanation: Self-pollination occurs when pollen transfers to the stigma of the same flower (autogamy) or another flower on the same plant (geitonogamy).

questions.question: The process by which seeds are transported away from the parent plant to prevent overcrowding is called:
questions.explanation: Seed dispersal is the movement of seeds away from the parent plant by wind, water, animals, or explosive mechanisms, reducing competition and aiding colonization of new areas.

questions.question: The maximum number of individuals of a species that an environment can sustainably support is called the:
questions.explanation: Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size that an environment can support given available resources (food, water, space, shelter).

questions.question: The first line of defence against pathogens in the human body includes:
questions.explanation: The first line of defence includes physical and chemical barriers: skin, mucous membranes, tears, saliva, and stomach acid that prevent pathogens from entering the body.

questions.question: Mineral deficiency of iron in the diet leads to:
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency causes anaemia — insufficient haemoglobin in red blood cells, resulting in reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, fatigue, and pallor.

questions.question: Iodine deficiency in the diet causes:
questions.explanation: Iodine is needed for thyroid hormone (thyroxine) production. Deficiency causes the thyroid gland to enlarge (goitre) as it tries to produce more hormone.

questions.question: The process by which the liver converts excess amino acids into urea is called:
questions.explanation: Deamination is the process in the liver where excess amino acids have their amino group (-NH₂) removed, forming ammonia, which is then converted to urea via the ornithine cycle.

questions.question: The protein responsible for carrying oxygen in red blood cells is:
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin (Hb) is the iron-containing protein in red blood cells that binds oxygen in the lungs (forming oxyhaemoglobin) and releases it to body tissues.

questions.question: The cerebrum is primarily responsible for:

options.C: consciousness, intelligence, memory, and voluntary movement

questions.explanation: The cerebrum (cerebral cortex) is the largest part of the brain, responsible for higher functions: consciousness, intelligence, memory, personality, and voluntary muscle movement.

questions.question: The neurotransmitter released at the neuromuscular junction that causes muscle contraction is:
questions.explanation: Acetylcholine (ACh) is the neurotransmitter released at the neuromuscular junction (where motor neurons meet muscle fibers), triggering muscle contraction.

questions.question: The geographical isolation of populations that leads to speciation is an example of:
questions.explanation: Allopatric speciation occurs when populations are geographically separated (by mountains, oceans, etc.), preventing gene flow, and diverge into separate species over time.

questions.question: The part of a flower that produces pollen grains (male gametes) is the:
questions.explanation: The anther is the male reproductive part of the flower that produces and releases pollen grains containing the male gametes. The anther sits on a stalk called the filament.

questions.question: Cancer is characterized by uncontrolled cell division. This occurs due to mutations in genes that normally:

options.B: regulate the cell cycle (oncogenes and tumour suppressor genes)

questions.explanation: Cancer arises when mutations affect genes that regulate the cell cycle: oncogenes (normally promote growth) or tumour suppressor genes (normally inhibit growth) like p53.

questions.question: Commensalism is a relationship in which:

options.B: one organism benefits while the other is harmed
options.C: one organism benefits while the other is neither harmed nor helped

questions.explanation: Commensalism benefits one organism while the other is unaffected. Example: barnacles on whales benefit from transport; whales are unaffected.

questions.question: Polyploidy refers to organisms that have:

options.B: more than two complete sets of chromosomes

questions.explanation: Polyploidy is the condition of having more than two complete sets of chromosomes (e.g., triploid 3n, tetraploid 4n). It is common in plants and important in crop breeding.

questions.question: Which of the following plants is an example of a monocotyledon?
questions.explanation: Maize is a monocotyledon — it has one seed leaf, parallel leaf veins, fibrous roots, and flower parts in multiples of three. Bean, mango, and tomato are dicotyledons.

questions.question: Which organ produces digestive enzymes including amylase, lipase, and proteases, and also produces insulin?
questions.explanation: The pancreas has two functions: exocrine (producing digestive enzymes: amylase, lipase, trypsin) and endocrine (producing hormones: insulin and glucagon from islets of Langerhans).

questions.question: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) regulates water balance by:

options.B: causing the kidney tubules to reabsorb more water, producing concentrated urine
options.C: stimulating the liver to produce more urea

questions.explanation: ADH (vasopressin) is produced by the hypothalamus and stored in the pituitary. It increases the permeability of kidney collecting ducts to water, increasing reabsorption and producing concentrated urine.

questions.question: Which of the following types of connective tissue forms the skeleton of the ear and nose (flexible), and covers joint surfaces (articular)?
questions.explanation: Cartilage is a flexible connective tissue found in the ear, nose, trachea, and covering joint surfaces (articular cartilage). It is avascular (no blood vessels).

questions.question: Global warming is primarily caused by the increase of which gas in the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Rising levels of CO₂ (mainly from burning fossil fuels and deforestation) enhance the greenhouse effect, trapping heat in the atmosphere and causing global warming.

questions.question: Linked genes are genes that are located on the same chromosome and tend to:

options.B: be inherited together and not assort independently
options.D: always be expressed in the phenotype

questions.explanation: Linked genes are on the same chromosome and tend to be inherited together, violating Mendel's law of independent assortment (unless separated by crossing over).

questions.question: In humans, the sex of the offspring is determined by:

options.B: the father's sex chromosome in the sperm (X or Y)

questions.explanation: Human sex is determined by the father's sperm: sperm carrying Y chromosome → male (XY); sperm carrying X chromosome → female (XX). Females are XX; males are XY.

questions.question: The cell theory states that:

options.A: all cells have the same structure and function
options.B: all living organisms are composed of cells, and cells arise only from pre-existing cells
options.D: plant cells are larger than animal cells

questions.explanation: The cell theory (Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow) states: (1) all living things are made of cells, (2) cells are the basic unit of life, (3) all cells come from pre-existing cells.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a renewable natural resource?
questions.explanation: Solar energy is a renewable resource because it is continuously replenished by the sun. Coal, natural gas, and petroleum are non-renewable fossil fuels.
